item.title: ‘America Doesn’t Want My Children or Grandchildren’
item.description: The Cruz family spent years building a life in New York. Then the risks of staying became too great.

item.title: Pete Buttigieg in the Wilderness
item.description: He has a beard, a splitting maul, and a house in Michigan. Is that enough to convince America that he’s a man of the people?

item.title: The Pete Hegseth Exception
item.description: Nearly a year after a national-security scandal erupted on my iPhone, no one in the Trump administration has faced consequences.

item.title: Sucker
item.description: My year as a degenerate gambler

item.title: Inside the Dirty, Dystopian World of AI Data Centers
item.description: The race to power AI is already remaking the physical world.

item.title: When Mentally Ill Teenagers Ask to Be Put to Death
item.description: A Dutch psychiatrist gave lethal injections to patients with mental suffering, some of them teenagers. Does that make him a hero—or something else?

item.title: What 100 Million Volts Do to the Body and Mind
item.description: The odds of being struck by lightning in America in a given year are one in 1.2 million. How does the experience reorient a person’s sense of chance, of fate?

item.title: The City Where Coetzee Is God
item.description: Searching for the Nobel laureate in Cape Town, the city he left behind

item.title: The Forgotten Female Pilots of World War II
item.description: The WASPs risked their lives flying for the Army. But for decades, the U.S. government refused to recognize their military service.

item.title: ‘I’m Far Angrier’
item.description: Can Cory Booker, once the candidate of love, run for president and stay true to who he is?
